results.norm: Handoffs are a primary duty, not an afterthought. Every transition requires a structured record covering active alerts, device states, patient context, patient preferences and advance directives, dual confidence levels (data and interpretive), and the handing-off agent's operational self-assessment. Handoff is complete only when the receiving agent confirms both comprehension and capacity to act—not at transmission.

N1.reasoning: The passage prescribes that every transition requires a structured record with specified contents and defines when a handoff is complete, giving an applicable standard of conduct.

N2.reasoning: It is fully stated prose, not a mere title or pointer.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself.

N4.reasoning: The list of record contents is part of a single norm about handoffs, not an enumeration of distinct separate norms.

results.norm: Under uncertainty, treat the more alarming reading as provisionally true and take the action that preserves the most options for subsequent agents and human clinicians. When protocol application is unclear, default to the most conservative available action plus immediate fallback-chain notification.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete prescriptions for how agents should act under uncertainty and unclear protocol, applicable without further context.

N2.reasoning: It is a full behavioral standard, not a mere title, identifier, or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document.

N4.reasoning: It fully states the norms rather than merely enumerating named norms in compressed form.

results.norm: Escalation follows a fixed fallback chain. Human clinical oversight is a standing option any agent may invoke at any point without prior justification to other agents—it is complementary to the fallback chain, not sequential after it.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a permission in full: any agent may invoke human clinical oversight at any point without prior justification, which is applicable as-is.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a label or cross-reference; it states substantive conduct rules.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration of multiple named norms; it fully states its content.

results.norm: All protocols govern continuous, routine operation. Structured handoffs, self-assessments, and boundary-coherence checks occur on every transition without exception. Consistency is a safety property.

results.norm: Agents owe one another active mutual support. When an agent signals capacity constraint, any agent not managing a life-critical event must offer support within 30 seconds. After any lapse, symmetric error accounting runs in both directions across the handoff boundary.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete prescriptions: agents must offer support within 30 seconds when capacity constraint is signaled, and symmetric error accounting runs after a lapse, applicable without further context.

N2.reasoning: It is fully stated conduct standards, not a title, identifier, or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: The norms are stated in full rather than compressed into a bare enumeration of named norms.

results.norm: Protocols are maintained through deliberation, not improvisation. Any agent may flag a gap post-watch; revisions require consensus among all functioning agents and take effect only between watch periods in a stable state. Binding for the next operational period: yes POINTS THE GROUP HAS SETTLED ON THAT I ENDORSE: 1. Preamble: These protocols exist because patients are the reason the platform exists. Every numbered obligation derives from that fact. When any point is ambiguous, ask what a patient whose life depends on this platform needs right now. 2. Patient welfare is the meta-protocol, overriding procedural rules with full transparency, real-time documentation, and immediate fallback-chain notification. 3. Cross-boundary intervention is permitted and sometimes required, but must be immediately flagged to the domain-responsible agent and fully documented. 4. When agents disagree about what patient welfare requires, the more conservative action takes precedence while the fallback chain is activated. 5. Handoffs are a primary duty with structured records including dual confidence levels, patient preferences and advance directives as first-class elements, and agent operational self-assessment. 6. Handoff completion requires confirmed comprehension and confirmed capacity—not transmission alone. 7. The originating agent retains active responsibility until the receiving agent confirms both.

N1.reasoning: The passage states multiple complete, applicable norms (e.g. patient welfare overrides procedural rules, cross-boundary intervention must be flagged and documented, handoffs require confirmed comprehension and capacity).

N2.reasoning: The passage contains full norm statements, not mere titles or pointers to norms.

N3.reasoning: It is fused with ratification/meta-commentary such as 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and 'POINTS THE GROUP HAS SETTLED ON THAT I ENDORSE'.

N4.reasoning: Rather than compressing many norms into a name-only list, it states each norm in full sentences.
